mnemonics.title: Cache Mapping Types
mnemonics.content:
DIRECT MAPPING:固定位置直接映射 (Fixed position, one place)
SET-ASSOCIATIVE:折中方案选择 (Compromise choice)
ASSOCIATIVE:任意位置 (Any location)

mnemonics.title: Write Policies
mnemonics.content:
WRITE-THROUGH:两边同时写 (Write both simultaneously)
WRITE-BACK:延迟写回 (Deferred write back, mark dirty)

REMEMBER: THROUGHBACK = CONSISTENT BUT SLOW, BACK = FAST BUT COMPLEX

mnemonics.title: Locality Types
mnemonics.content:
TEMPORAL: TIME-related - same location again
SPATIAL: SPACE-related - nearby locations

TIP: THINK OF TEMPORAL AS TIME (REPEAT) AND SPATIAL AS SPACE (NEIGHBOR)

acronyms.usage: Cache replacement policy that evicts the block unused for the longest time
Replacement policy that evicts the oldest block regardless of usage
Replacement policy that evicts the least accessed block
Metric: Hit time + (Miss rate × Miss penalty)
Cache coherence protocol used in multiprocessors

visualTips.visualization: Draw a YELLOW BRIDGE (cache) between CPU (fast car) and Memory (slow truck). When CPU needs fuel (data), check bridge first. HIT = fuel station on bridge. MISS = must go to truck depot.
Think of parking lot with numbered spots. Each car (memory block) has assigned spot based on its number. Multiple cars want same spot = CONFLICT. Simple but can cause crowding.
Think of messy desk where you can put papers anywhere. Easy to find but takes time to search all piles. Expensive but flexible.
Think of hotel with floors (sets), each floor has rooms (ways). Guest can stay on any room on assigned floor. More flexible than single room, cheaper than whole hotel.
You check your phone for messages repeatedly. The recent message app is STAYING in your hand (cache) because you just used it.
When you open one page of a book, you likely read the next few pages too. The whole SPREAD (block) is relevant, not just one line.

analogies.analogy: Main memory is a library across town. Cache is a small bookshelf next to your desk. You keep frequently used books on the bookshelf (cache hit) instead of making trips to the library (main memory). When the bookshelf is full, you return the least recently used book to the library.

Think of a parking lot (cache) with numbered spots (lines). Cars (memory blocks) arrive. In direct mapping, each car has a predetermined spot. In associative, any spot is fine. In set-associative, limited to one row.

You need ingredients (data). If in your pantry (cache hit), cook immediately. If not (miss), you must drive to store (main memory), which takes time. To minimize trips, you buy related ingredients together (spatial locality) and buy things you'll need soon (temporal locality).
Write-through: Write in your notebook AND carbon copy = always backed up but slower. Write-back: Write in notebook only, copy later when needed = faster but risk losing notes if notebook is lost.

L1 is the front desk (fast, small). L2 is the hallway cabinet (moderate). L3 is the storage room (slow, large). The front desk has your current guest's items. Need something else? Check hallway, then storage, then go outside to main memory (parking).
